title: Isolating and removing a radiator
subtitle: Drain a single radiator and remove it from the wall without draining the system
excerpt: How to isolate and remove a single radiator: closing the lockshield and thermostatic valves, connecting a hose to drain the radiator without draining the whole system, disconnecting both valve bodies, and lifting the radiator off its brackets.

glossaryTerms.definition: The valve at the return (outlet) end of a radiator, usually covered by a plastic cap. It is pre-set at installation to balance the flow rate through the radiator and is not normally used as a day-to-day control. To isolate a radiator, note the number of turns it takes to close, so it can be returned to the same position when reinstatement.

The self-actuating valve at the flow (inlet) end of a radiator that opens and closes in response to room temperature. To isolate a radiator, the TRV is closed to its minimum (winter off) setting; on most valves this is achieved by turning the head fully clockwise.

content.text: A single radiator can be isolated and drained without touching the boiler or draining the rest of the heating system, as long as both the
can be closed fully. Switch the boiler off and allow the system water to cool before starting; central heating water at operating temperature is around 70 to 80 degrees Celsius.

Close the TRV head fully clockwise to the minimum or off position. Remove the plastic cap from the lockshield valve. Count the number of turns it takes to close the lockshield fully and note it down; you will open it by the same number of turns on reinstatement to restore the original balance setting. Close the lockshield fully clockwise.

Place dust sheets and a bucket or drain hose under one of the valve connections. Using an adjustable spanner, loosen the union nut connecting the TRV valve body to the radiator tail. Loosen slowly: water will begin to flow as the nut releases. Have the bucket ready. To drain faster, open the bleed valve at the top of the radiator with a bleed key to allow air in. Drain until the radiator is empty.

With the radiator empty, remove the union nut at the TRV end completely. Place a cloth under the lockshield end and loosen that union nut. The radiator tail may spin in the radiator; hold the tail with water-pump pliers while undoing the union nut. Both valve bodies remain connected to the pipework; only the union connections to the radiator tails are disconnected.
Step 4, lift the radiator off the wall

Lift the radiator upward and slightly toward you to disengage the top brackets from their wall hooks. Most radiators hook onto two brackets at the top; some also have a bottom bracket. Have a second pair of hands available for any radiator longer than 1200 mm (4 ft) or wider than 450 mm (18 in); the combined weight and awkward balance make solo lifting unsafe. Prop the radiator on a flat surface before moving.

To rehang: hook the radiator onto the brackets, reconnect both union nuts hand-tight then a further half-turn. Open the lockshield valve by the same number of turns noted at the start. Open the TRV. Open the bleed valve until water appears and close it. Check for leaks at both union nuts. Restore the boiler.

attrs.symptom: Water continues to flow when the TRV is fully closed
attrs.cause: The TRV head is seized open, or the valve pin is stuck in the open position.
attrs.fix: Remove the TRV head. The valve pin should depress easily with a fingernail; if it is stuck down, press and release it several times. If it will not close, the TRV valve body needs replacing before the radiator can be isolated.

attrs.symptom: Union nut will not loosen despite firm spanner pressure
attrs.cause: The union has corroded and the threads are seized; common on systems over 20 years old.
attrs.fix: Apply penetrating oil to the union thread and leave for 20 minutes. Use a second spanner to hold the valve body stationary while the union nut is turned. Do not apply heat to copper heating pipework.
